checks.a_analysis: The output follows the source document's structure and content closely. No fabricated introductory or concluding remarks, nor invented section headings or paragraphs were identified. The reading order generally aligns with the logical flow of the minutes.
I verified all physical addresses and program names. No fabricated contact information (phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs) was introduced in the output.

All nine major numbered sections and all six pages of the source PDF are represented in the output. While some specific content within sections is missing (addressed in C5), no entire sections or pages were omitted.
The source document does not contain any tables; it consists entirely of structured text and lists.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output has several notable omissions. In Section 3, sub-headings 'a. 911/NG911 in the Media' and 'b. AddressNC and Census Geospatial Data' are missing. In the first paragraph of page 3 (Section 3 continuation), a reference URL is omitted. Most significantly, on page 5 in Section 6, the three technical sub-bullets under the item 'Source data questions:' are entirely absent. These sub-bullets contained specific questions about satellite, color infrared, and NAIP imagery, which are critical technical details of the discussion. The cumulative loss of these specific points and the reference link constitutes a significant omission.
checks.b_evidence_source: Source Page 5, Section 6, list under 'Source data questions:': 'o Is current satellite imagery (applied in 1996) suitable as the source for classification? o Would color infrared imagery, if funded and processed from statewide imagery acquisition, be the best source for statewide land cover classification? o Would National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) leaf-on imagery be adequate for statewide land cover classification?' Also Section 3 sub-headings and the URL on page 3.
checks.b_evidence_output: The list element ends abruptly at the term 'Source data questions:' (reading_order: 43).

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: A minor wording difference was found on page 1 where a word was dropped, slightly altering the grammar of a sentence, although the meaning remains mostly clear.
checks.b_evidence_source: ...once the national standard is in place, what would be the utility of a North Carolina specific schema?
checks.b_evidence_output: ...once the national standard is in place, what would the utility of a North Carolina specific schema?

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output against the source document. No text blocks or paragraphs appear to be duplicated. The output correctly follows the sequence of the document across page breaks without repeating headers or footers in the main text content.

The heading hierarchy for the main numbered sections of the document is inconsistent. In the source document, items 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 are all primary sections at the same level of hierarchy. However, in the output, sections 4 and 7 are assigned level H2, while sections 5, 6, 8, and 9 are assigned level H3. This is an incorrect nesting/leveling of logically equivalent sections.
checks.b_evidence_source: 4. Working Group for Professional Land Surveying and GIS; 5. GICC Website; 6. Committee Reports; 7. Council Items; 8. Other Items; 9. Future Meeting Dates
checks.b_evidence_output: element 21: 'level': 2 (4.); element 33: 'level': 3 (5.); element 38: 'level': 3 (6.); element 44: 'level': 2 (7.); element 51: 'level': 3 (8.); element 54: 'level': 3 (9.)

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no tables.
The reading order in the output correctly reflects the linear flow of the source document, following the text logically from page to page and within sections.

The list structure is corrupted in several ways. 1) The primary numbered list (1-9) is fragmented: items 1-3 are in a list object, while items 4-9 are treated as separate headings/paragraphs. 2) Subsection headings 'a. 911/NG911 in the Media' (page 1) and 'b. AddressNC and Census Geospatial Data' (page 1) are entirely missing from the output. 3) The nested bulleted list under 'Source data questions:' on page 5 (starting with 'o Is current satellite imagery...') is completely omitted from the output.
checks.b_evidence_source: a. 911/NG911 in the Media; b. AddressNC and Census Geospatial Data; o Is current satellite imagery (applied in 1996) suitable as the source for classification?; o Would color infrared imagery, if funded and processed from statewide imagery acquisition, be the best source for statewide land cover classification?; o Would National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) leaf-on imagery be adequate for statewide land cover classification?
checks.b_evidence_output: (Text not found in output elements 6, 8, or 43)
checks.b_evidence_location: Page 1, Section 3a/3b; Page 5, Section 6, last bullet point (element 43)

checks.a_analysis: The URL on page 3 is correctly transcribed as text in element 19. While the schema does not explicitly represent this as a hyperlink object, the text content is accurate and there are no broken or fabricated links present.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the single-column flow and overall organization of the source PDF. The hierarchical structure of headings, subheadings, and paragraphs is preserved. Information is located in the same relative position and sequence as in the source document. The presence of a Table of Contents sidebar for navigation is an expected design feature and does not constitute a layout failure.
The source document does not contain any tables; it is composed entirely of text paragraphs and lists. Therefore, there are no tables to misrender.
The source PDF is a text-only document without any meaningful images, charts, or logos. The HTML output correctly reflects this absence.

There is no duplication of body content such as paragraphs or list items. While headings appear in both the sidebar Table of Contents and the main body of the document, this is an intentional accessibility feature and not a duplication defect.
